content.problemStatement:
There is a chessboard with NNN rows (numbered 111 through NNN) and NNN columns (numbered 111 through NNN). MMM squares on this chessboard (numbered 111 through MMM) are marked. For each valid iii, the iii-th of the marked squares is in the rir_iri​-th row and cic_ici​-th column.

You should perform the following process:


First, you take a rook and put this rook on one of the marked squares.
Then you move the rook M−1M-1M−1 times; since it is a rook, each of these moves must be to a marked square in the same row or the same column as the square where the rook was currently standing.
The rook must visit each of the marked squares exactly once. In each move, squares strictly between those where the rook stands immediately before and after this move are not visited by the rook.
Also, the directions of the moves must alternate ― you may not move the rook in the same row twice or in the same column twice in any two consecutive moves.
Find one valid path for the rook that visits the marked squares. It is guaranteed that at least one such path exists. If there are multiple solutions, you may find any one of them.
constraints: 1≤ri,ci≤N1 \le r_i, c_i \le N1≤ri​,ci​≤N for each valid iii
content.inputFormat:
The first line of the input contains a single integer TTT denoting the number of test cases. The description of TTT test cases follows.
The first line of each test case contains two space-separated integers NNN and MMM.
MMM lines follow. For each valid iii, the iii-th of these lines contains two space-separated integers rir_iri​ and cic_ici​.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print a single line containing MMM space-separated integers ― the indices of marked squares in the order in which the rook should visit them. These integers should form a permutation of 1,2,…,M1, 2, \ldots, M1,2,…,M.
